homePage.title: Learn more about body and health with Sunn Utøver
homePage.description: Sunn Utøver is a platform that makes information related to the condition Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (REDs) more accessible to athletes, coaches, and parents. Here you will learn what REDs is, the typical signs of this condition, and what can be done to both manage and prevent REDs.

screen_01.title: Learning Objectives
screen_01.subtitle: After this module, you should be able to:
list_items1: Explain what Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (REDs) is.
Define the term Low Energy Availability (LEA) and know that LEA is the main cause of REDs.
Distinguish between short-term LEA and long-term/problematic LEA.
Mention which body systems and functions can be affected when the body receives too little energy.

screen_02.title: The body needs energy for more than just training
screen_02.description1: For the body to function optimally, it needs enough energy - not just for training, but to maintain vital daily functions. Energy is used for, among other things:
list_items1: Brain function and nervous system
Heart and circulatory system
Hormone production
Immune system
Bone health
Digestion and metabolism

screen_03.title: Energy Availability
screen_03.description1: Energy availability is about how much energy (food and drink) the body has left to run all its functions after the energy used for physical activity is subtracted.
screen_03.description2: Energy balance is another important concept to understand when talking about energy availability, and can simply be explained as a state where energy intake equals energy expenditure.

screen_04.title: Energy Balance
screen_04.description1: Energy balance is another important concept to understand when talking about energy availability, and can simply be explained as a state where energy intake equals energy expenditure.

screen_05.title: Negative Energy Balance
screen_05.description1: Any deviation from this balance, whether conscious or unconscious, can lead to low energy availability more easily than many of us think.

screen_06.title: Low Energy Availability
screen_06.description1: Low energy availability occurs when the body receives too little energy to cover both training needs and basic requirements like breathing, circulating blood, building bone and tissue, producing hormones, etc.
screen_06.subtitle: 🪫 Think of it like a battery in power-saving mode:
screen_06.description2: When the body gets too little energy, it must prioritize what it uses the energy for. It will then reduce 'non-essential' functions to conserve resources.

bg_info_box.title: Difference from energy balance: You can be in energy balance (i.e., not gaining or losing weight) but still have low energy availability if the training volume is high and the food intake does not cover the body's needs after exercise.

screen_07.title: Adaptive vs. Problematic LEA
screen_07.description1: It is normal for the body to adapt to short periods of low energy availability. This is called adaptive LEA and is not necessarily a problem. If such energy deficits happen often enough (several days a week, over several weeks), the body may start saving energy and deprioritize functions such as hormone production and recovery.
screen_07.description2: There is a big difference between having a 'tired week' and a body that consistently lacks the energy to function as it should.

screen_08.title: From Low Energy Availability (LEA) to Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (REDs)
screen_08.description1: REDs occurs when the body has had too low energy availability over time to function normally. This reduces the ability to regulate important systems, which can lead to health problems.
screen_08.description2: REDs is not just about body size or weight, but about the imbalance between energy intake and expenditure. One can have a normal or high body weight and still be in low energy availability - especially if energy expenditure through training is high.
screen_08.description3: REDs affects both boys and girls - and does not only apply to elite athletes. Recreational athletes can also develop REDs if their energy needs are not met.

screen_09.title: Low Energy Availability even in Energy Balance?
screen_09.description1: You can be in total energy balance over the course of a day - and still risk low energy availability.
screen_09.description2: Imagine you train early and eat little before and after. The body then enters a deficit for many hours, even if the daily total is sufficient.
screen_09.description3: It is therefore not just the amount of energy that matters - timing and consistent energy supply are crucial. If you eat too little around training sessions, you are at risk of developing problematic energy availability and REDs.

screen_10.title: Quiz
screen_10.question: What does it mean for the body to be in low energy availability (LEA)?
screen_10.options1: That you eat too little fat
screen_10.options2: That the body uses more energy than remains after training

screen_11.title: Quiz
screen_11.question: Which of these statements is true?
screen_11.options1: REDs only occurs in athletes with very low body weight
screen_11.options2: REDs can affect both boys and girls
screen_11.options3: REDs is primarily about being out of shape
screen_11.options4: REDs is only a problem in elite sports

screen_01.title: Learning Objectives
screen_01.subtitle: After this module, you should be able to:
list_items1: Identify both health and performance consequences of REDs.
Explain how REDs can affect different bodily systems in the long term.
Be aware of psychological consequences.

screen_02.title: The body must prioritize
screen_02.description1: As we learned in the previous module: when the body doesn't get enough energy over time, it must begin to prioritize which functions are most important. This means that functions that are not immediately vital are turned down.
screen_02.subtitle: This has consequences for both health and performance.

screen_03.title: Impeded growth, puberty, and hormone function
screen_03.description1: When the body gets too little energy, it turns down signals that control growth, puberty, and hormone production, which can delay puberty. Girls may lose their period, boys get lower testosterone levels, and hormones for metabolism, mood, and appetite are also affected.
screen_03.subtitle: Why it matters
screen_03.description2: Hormones are crucial for growth, bone health, and fertility. Without enough energy, the body cannot build itself strong enough, but with early help and proper nutrition, development can often be caught up.

screen_04.title: Weakened bone health
screen_04.description1: When the body gets too little energy, vital hormones that build bone are reduced. This makes the skeleton weaker, increasing the risk of stress fractures and osteoporosis.
screen_04.subtitle: Why it matters
screen_04.description2: Around 90% of bone mass is built before age 18 for girls and 20 for boys. If you don't build enough bone mass in your youth, the risk of osteoporosis and stress fractures increases - both today and as an adult.

bg_info_box.title:
Did you know...
Around 90% of peak bone mass is built by the time you reach your 20s? This lays the foundation for strong bones for life - and the body needs enough energy during youth to achieve it.

screen_05.title: Impaired gastrointestinal function
screen_05.description1: When the body gets too little energy, digestion is deprioritized. This means the stomach works slower, leading to reduced appetite, constipation, bloating, and discomfort. Many also experience stomach issues similar to Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS).
screen_05.subtitle: Why it matters
screen_05.description2: Good digestion is important for absorbing nutrients, keeping energy levels up, and feeling well in everyday life. Long-term digestive problems can lead to reduced quality of life, poorer recovery, and less appetite.

screen_06.title: Impaired energy metabolism
screen_06.description1: When the body gets too little energy over time, it lowers the metabolism to save strength.
screen_06.subtitle: Why it matters
screen_06.description2: Low metabolism causes the body to use less energy at rest. This can lead to increased fatigue, chills, weight changes, and hormonal imbalances.

screen_07.title: Impaired blood function
screen_07.description1: With low energy availability, the body may get too little iron and other vital nutrients. Iron is necessary to produce red blood cells, which carry oxygen around the body. When iron stores are low (common among young female athletes), the blood's ability to deliver oxygen efficiently is reduced.
screen_07.subtitle: Why it matters
screen_07.description2: Iron deficiency can worsen energy deficiency because it affects metabolism and makes the body use energy less efficiently, both at rest and during activity. Iron deficiency in REDs can also weaken hormone balance and affect bone health, fertility, and mental health.

screen_08.title: Urinary incontinence
screen_08.description1: REDs reduces the energy supply to muscles, including the pelvic floor, which is responsible for keeping the bladder tight during physical exertion. Hormonal changes - especially low estrogen in girls - can also weaken the supporting tissue in the urinary tract.
screen_08.subtitle: Why it matters
screen_08.description2: Weak pelvic floor muscles and altered connective tissue function can lead to urinary leakage during running, jumping, or strength training.

screen_09.title: Impaired regulation of blood sugar and lipids
screen_09.description1: Too little energy causes hormones that control blood sugar and metabolism (including insulin, cortisol, and leptin) to become imbalanced.
screen_09.subtitle: Why it matters
screen_09.description2: When these hormones are imbalanced, it can lead to unstable blood sugar, energy fluctuations, dizziness, and headaches. This affects both performance and mood, and makes it harder for the body to use food effectively.

screen_10.title: Mental health issues
screen_10.description1: REDs affects the brain through energy deficiency and hormonal disturbances. This can change brain chemistry and make it harder to regulate mood.
screen_10.subtitle: Why it matters
screen_10.description2: One may experience depression, anxiety, or irritability. Mental health issues can occur even without body pressure or conscious food control, making it harder to have energy for daily life, train well, and feel like oneself.

screen_11.title: Impaired brain function
screen_11.description1: In REDs, the brain receives too little energy, and the hormones that affect concentration, learning, and reaction time become imbalanced.
screen_11.subtitle: Why it matters
screen_11.description2: This can make it harder to focus, remember information, and make quick decisions – both in sports and at school. One may feel foggy-headed and 'out of it,' even without being physically tired.

screen_12.title: Sleep problems
screen_12.description1: Research shows that athletes who get too little energy relative to how much they train also sleep worse. They wake up more often during the night, have lower sleep efficiency, and sleep less in the deep, vital sleep phase. Researchers believe this may be linked to how the body reacts to low energy - for example, through hormones that affect sleep and wakefulness.
screen_12.subtitle: Why it matters
screen_12.description2: Sleep is absolutely crucial for recovery, performance, and avoiding injuries. Poor sleep can be a sign that the body is not getting enough energy, especially when training hard.

screen_13.title: Impaired heart function
screen_13.description1: Severe REDs is associated with lower blood pressure and resting heart rate.
screen_13.subtitle: Why it matters
screen_13.description2: Reduced cardiac activity can be a result of energy deficiency and may increase the risk of heart rhythm disturbances.

screen_14.title: Reduced skeletal muscle function
screen_14.description1: Low energy availability reduces muscle protein synthesis. This is due to hormonal changes and insufficient supply of both energy and nutrients like protein and carbohydrates. At the same time, the muscle's storage of glycogen, an important energy source during exercise, is reduced. The result is weakened muscle strength, poorer recovery, and reduced ability to adapt to training load over time.
screen_14.subtitle: Why it matters
screen_14.description2: One feels weaker and has less stamina, which impairs performance and increases the risk of injuries like strains and overuse.

screen_15.title: Weakened immune system
screen_15.description1: The body needs energy and nutrients to make immune cells and fight disease. In REDs, the immune system is put in power-saving mode.
screen_15.subtitle: Why it matters
screen_15.description2: One gets sick more often, has longer illness durations, and can tolerate less training before the body says stop. Infections and inflammation can also last longer than normal.

screen_16.title: How does REDs affect performance?
screen_16.description1: You have now learned how the body prioritizes away important functions when energy is insufficient. These functions naturally also have consequences for performance.
screen_16.subtitle: Common performance consequences of REDs are:
list_items1: Stagnation - progress stops
Increased training wear - training feels heavier
Poorer recovery
More illness and injuries

screen_17.title: Quiz
screen_17.question: When in life is approximately 90% of peak bone mass built up?

screen_18.title: Quiz
screen_18.question: Which of these is not a common health consequence of REDs?

modul3Page.main_title: Module 3 - What are the symptoms of REDs?

screen_01.title: Learning Objectives
screen_01.subtitle: After this module, you should be able to:
list_items1: Recognize physical and psychological symptoms that can occur with REDs
Understand how symptoms can vary from person to person
Know which signs to look out for in yourself or others
Be more confident in when it may be wise to take symptoms seriously and seek help

screen_02.title: The body speaks out
screen_02.description1: When the body gets too little energy over time, it tries to adapt, which can have several consequences. We learned about many of these in the previous module. These will manifest themselves as bodily symptoms to a greater or lesser degree.
screen_02.description2: Symptoms are the body's way of speaking out. Taking them seriously is important.
screen_02.description3: In this module we will go through various physical and psychological symptoms of REDs.

bg_info_box.title: Why is it important to understand the symptoms?
bg_info_box.subtitle: REDs can develop gradually without you noticing. Therefore, it's important to be aware of which parameters you should monitor regularly. That way:
list_items: You can take action early
You can protect both health and performance

screen_03.title: Unexplained fatigue / low energy levels
screen_03.description1: If you feel tired without understanding why, or notice that your surplus energy disappears, it can be a sign that your body isn't getting enough energy to cover what you use.
screen_03.description2: This can be an early signal that the body is out of balance. Logging training, sleep, mood, and energy levels regularly can make it easier to spot patterns.

bg_info_box.title: Tips:
list_items: Use a diary to monitor both training load and energy levels.
For long-lasting fatigue, consider speaking with someone you feel comfortable with.
Also monitor sleep and mood - they are often linked to energy levels.

screen_04.title: Are you often sick / does it take a long time to get well?
screen_04.description1: If the body gets too little energy over time, the immune system is deprioritized. This means you can get sick more easily, and it takes longer to fully recover.
screen_04.description2: Tracking how often you're sick and how quickly you recover can provide important hints about energy balance and recovery.

bg_info_box.title: Tips:
list_items: Use a diary to monitor both training load and energy levels.
For long-lasting fatigue, consider speaking with someone you feel comfortable with.
Also monitor sleep and mood - they are often linked to energy levels.

screen_05.title: Training stagnation - do you train more than you recover?
screen_05.description1: If you feel your fitness is at a standstill, it may be because the body isn't getting enough energy to absorb the training you're putting in.
screen_05.subtitle: Why is it important to monitor?
screen_05.description2: An imbalance between training, food, and rest means the body cannot recover. Then both progress and performance are absent. It can also make you more prone to injuries.

bg_info_box.title: Tips - what you can monitor:
list_items: How does training feel (heavy / light)?
Are you able to complete what you planned?
Are you making progress over time, or standing still?

screen_06.title: Vulnerable to injuries - do you experience minor pains or recurring injuries?
screen_06.description1: With REDs, the body's ability to build and repair tissue can be reduced. This makes muscles, tendons, and bones more prone to overuse injuries.
screen_06.subtitle: Why is it important to monitor?
screen_06.description2: Persistent or recurring injuries can be a signal that the body isn't getting what it needs to handle the training. Early insights can prevent serious problems.

bg_info_box.title: Tips and what you can monitor:
list_items: Increase intensity and duration gradually
Ensure regular food intake - especially after training
Do you often experience pain, do you often have to adjust training due to pain?

screen_07.title: Hormonal imbalances
screen_07.description1: Low energy availability can lead to hormonal imbalances. For girls, this can mean irregular or absent menstruation. For boys, it can manifest as lower testosterone levels – fewer morning erections, reduced libido, and low energy.
screen_07.subtitle: Why is it important to monitor?
screen_07.description2: Hormonal imbalances can have long-term consequences for bone health and development.

bg_info_box.title: Tips and what you can monitor:
list_items: For girls: keep an eye on changes in the menstrual cycle
For boys: fewer than 3 morning erections per week can be a sign of REDs
Take changes in the body seriously – seek help if you are unsure

screen_08.title: Feeling cold - do you get cold easier than before?
screen_08.description1: The body lowers its core temperature as an energy-saving strategy when it doesn't get enough nutrition. This can make you feel cold, especially in your hands and feet – even when it isn't cold around you.

bg_info_box.title: Tips and what you can monitor:
list_items: Notice if you feel abnormally cold often
Look at your food intake - the body needs fuel to stay warm

screen_09.title: Sleep problems - do you struggle to fall asleep or sleep through the night?
screen_09.description1: An energy deficit can cause the body stress - which can disrupt sleep. It's not uncommon to wake up early, sleep restlessly, or feel unrefreshed even after many hours in bed.
screen_09.subtitle: Why is it important to monitor?
screen_09.description2: Sleep is absolutely crucial for recovery, hormone balance, and performance. Without good sleep, the body cannot recover - which can worsen other symptoms.

bg_info_box.title: Tips and what you can monitor:
list_items: Notice your perceived sleep quality
Notice if you often wake up during the night
Notice if you feel rested in the morning

screen_10.title: Iron deficiency
screen_10.description1: Low energy intake and menstruation (in girls) can affect iron stores in the body. This can cause you to feel extra tired, short of breath, and perform worse.
screen_10.subtitle: Why is it important to monitor?
screen_10.description2: Iron deficiency can cause symptoms similar to normal fatigue – but has a completely different cause. It should be taken seriously and possibly followed up with blood tests.

bg_info_box.title: Tips and what you can monitor:
list_items: Note if you experience a lot of dizziness or headaches
Eat iron-rich food together with vitamin C (e.g., orange juice) for better absorption
Avoid coffee and tea right before and after meals if you suspect iron deficiency

screen_11.title: Stomach and intestinal issues - Bloated? Gas? Upset stomach?
screen_11.description1: Many experience changes in the stomach with REDs - such as sluggish or loose stools, pain, or general unrest. This can be due to both low energy intake and stress around food and the body.
screen_11.subtitle: Why is it important to monitor?
screen_11.description2: Stomach problems can be a reaction to stress and deficiency. When digestion slows down, nutrient absorption is also affected.

bg_info_box.title: Tips and what you can monitor:
list_items: Notice stomach pain, bloating, nausea or gas
Bowel function (frequency, consistency, discomfort)
Connection with stress, meals or training?

screen_12.title: Mood swings, depression and irritability
screen_12.description1: When the body lacks energy over time, it affects the nervous system and hormone balance - which can lead to being easily irritated, losing motivation, or feeling down for no obvious reason.
screen_12.subtitle: Why is it important to monitor?
screen_12.description2: Mental health is closely linked to physical surplus energy, and it is important to take feelings seriously - especially when it affects training, school, or everyday joy.

bg_info_box.title: Tips and what you can monitor:
list_items: Notice patterns - e.g. if you are often irritated, or feel down without anything special having happened.
Compare mood with sleep, energy, and training in a diary - it can provide useful insights.
Talk to someone you trust - it could be parents, a coach, friend, or school nurse.

screen_13.title: Poor concentration and reduced mental sharpness
screen_13.description1: The brain needs a lot of energy to function optimally. When the energy supply is too low over time, the body deprioritizes cognitive functions like focus, memory, and problem solving.
screen_13.description2: Concentration difficulties make it harder to perform at school, in training, and in social situations. It can feel frustrating not to be 'oneself' mentally, and it is often an early signal that something is out of balance.

bg_info_box.title: Tips and what you can monitor:
list_items: Notice patterns - e.g. if you are often irritated, or feel down without anything special having happened.
Compare mood with sleep, energy, and training in a diary - it can provide useful insights.
Talk to someone you trust - it could be parents, a coach, friend, or school nurse.

screen_14.title: Anxiety or unrest around food and eating
screen_14.description1: Some may experience stress around food - either because they feel they have to eat “perfectly”, they feel they have to “earn” food, they feel guilty if they eat more than usual, or they avoid certain types of food.
screen_14.subtitle: When food creates unrest in everyday life, it is a sign that something is out of balance.

bg_info_box.title: Tips and what you can monitor:
list_items: Try to tune in: do you eat when you're hungry - and until you're full?
Ask yourself: Do I think about food more than before? Do I feel stressed eating certain things?
It can be useful to talk to a safe adult or professional - especially if thoughts affect everyday life.

screen_15.title: Compulsive exercise habits
screen_15.description1: Some feel unrest or a bad conscience if they don't train. This can lead to training even when the body needs rest, and ignoring signals like fatigue, pains/injuries, or illness.
screen_15.subtitle: Why is it important:
screen_15.description2: Rest is crucial to handle the training load and avoid injuries. If training always “has to be done”, even when the body says no, the risk of overload, injuries, and energy deficiency increases.

bg_info_box.title: Tips and what you can monitor:
list_items: It can be smart to take a break and tune in - do I have energy and desire, or do I feel that I “have to”?

screen_16.title: Social withdrawal
screen_16.description1: Withdrawing from friends, shared meals, and activities not related to training. It can become difficult to find energy for socializing - or one feels it "gets in the way" of training routines, food plans, or control needs.
screen_16.description2: Being with others provides joy, support, and a break from performance focus. When one isolates oneself, it becomes easier to get caught in negative thoughts and destructive patterns.

bg_info_box.title: Tips and what you can monitor:
list_items: Be aware of how often you say no to social things.
Make room for activities that are not just about training.

screen_17.title: Body dissatisfaction, perfectionism and low self-esteem
screen_17.description1: Body focus, comparison, and perfectionism can make you feel never good enough.
screen_17.description2: These are thoughts that can take up a lot of space and drain energy. They can also push you towards unhealthy choices and overriding the body's signals. It's easy to believe that "more is better" in sports – but the body needs enough energy to build itself stronger. REDs often affects highly goal-oriented athletes, who may not have learned how best to support the body with food, rest, and recovery.

screen_18.title: Experienced symptoms vary from person to person
screen_18.description1: Symptoms of REDs vary significantly from person to person. This is because the body's response to low energy availability (LEA) is affected by several individual factors such as:

inner_box1.title: Biological variation
list_items: Gender: Women and men have different hormone systems
Hormonal status: Some have more sensitive hormone systems - even a small energy deficit can cause large effects
Genetics: Some have “robust” biology that handles low energy better, while others can develop symptoms quickly
Previous health conditions: someone who has had eating disorders, osteoporosis, or prior hormone problems is more vulnerable to developing symptoms

inner_box2.title: Duration and severity of energy deficiency
list_items: Gender: Women and men have different hormone systems
Hormonal status: Some have more sensitive hormone systems - even a small energy deficit can cause large effects
Genetics: Some have “robust” biology that handles low energy better, while others can develop symptoms quickly
Previous health conditions: someone who has had eating disorders, osteoporosis, or prior hormone problems is more vulnerable to developing symptoms

inner_box3.title: Social factors
list_items: Normalization of warning signs: Athletes may overlook symptoms like sleep problems, hormone disturbances (like loss of period in girls) because they think it's “normal” in the sport
Role of the environment: Coaches, teammates, and the surrounding culture can affect how the athlete interprets and manages signals from the body

screen_01.title: Learning Objectives
screen_01.description1: Prevention is about everything that happens before things go too far, meaning to:
list_items1: Anticipate risks
Recognize early signs of low energy availability and REDs
Facilitate good habits
Promote safety and openness
screen_01.description2: This module will go through key aspects and best practices related to prevention, so that you as an athlete, coach, or parent are better equipped.

screen_02.title: Why prevent LEA and REDs?
list_items1: REDs often develops gradually, making it difficult to detect
REDs negatively affects both health and performance
Early intervention is more effective than treatment later
Many young athletes, coaches, and parents are unaware of the signals – this needs to change

bg_info_box.title: Continuously working preventively with REDs is crucial to protect the body’s development, health, and performance.

screen_03.title: Self-monitoring - Learn to understand your body
screen_03.description1: Paying attention to your body's signals is one of the most important things you can do to detect low energy availability and REDs early.

rows.col2: Stagnation/progress stops, low energy during sessions
Restless sleep, difficulty falling asleep, waking up tired
Frequent injuries, slow recovery, often sick
Skipping meals, forgetting to eat after training, eating less than usual

bg_info_box.title: Try to monitor these parameters weekly (this can be done in a diary or app).

screen_04.title: Follow trends
screen_04.description1: It is completely normal to have a bad day or week. What matters is tracking changes over time:
list_items1: Is your sleep gradually getting worse?
Do you feel tired week after week?
Has your mood been low for a long time?
Has menstruation been absent for several months?

rows.col2: Stagnation/progress stops, low energy during sessions
Restless sleep, difficulty falling asleep, waking up tired
Frequent injuries, slow recovery, often sick
Skipping meals, forgetting to eat after training, eating less than usual

bg_info_box.title: If negative trends persist over several weeks, they should be taken seriously.

screen_05.title: Observe and dare to ask questions
screen_05.subtitle: Coaches and parents play an important role in identifying low energy availability.

left: Am I constantly tired despite getting enough sleep?
Am I more sore than usual and struggling to recover between sessions?
Has my performance stopped improving without clear reason?
right: Do I often feel low or have mood swings?
Have I had repeated injuries this past year?
Have I had stress fractures or fatigue injuries?
Women: Is my menstrual cycle irregular or absent?
Men: Do I have lower libido or fewer morning erections?

bg_info_box.title: Note: Each sign may have causes other than REDs.

screen_06.title: Nutrition - it's about filling the battery
screen_06.description1: If you train heavily, you have a larger battery that you must fill up.
screen_06.description2: To fill this battery, it is important that we start with nutritious foods; which contain the building blocks we need for a solid foundation.
screen_06.description3: For someone who trains a lot and has high energy needs, it is sometimes also important to add extra, for example in the form of pastries, bars, sports drinks, chocolate, soda, and so on.

bg_info_box.title: High-energy food is not "unhealthy" for you when you train a lot, it is necessary to quickly refuel.

screen_07.title: Exercise - Training and energy needs
screen_07.description1: Take a look at your training week and ask yourself:
list_items1: Which days are hard / high training load?
Which days are easy?
Which days require extra fueling?

bg_info_box.title: This exercise is best done together with a parent and coach.

screen_08.title: Eat enough throughout the day
list_items1: Eat too little overall
Distribute food intake poorly throughout the day
Lack proper nutrition around training

icon_list_items.text: Do not cut carbohydrates – your body needs them

bg_info_box.title:
Is it difficult to eat something in the morning or right after training?
Then different types of drinks or liquid meals like smoothies and drinking yogurt can be good alternatives.

sub_list_items1.text: The higher the intensity - the more important carbohydrates become as fuel
With low carbohydrate access over time, the body can enter 'power-saving mode', with many of the same symptoms as REDs
Good habits with carbohydrates before, during, and after training are protective regarding:

bg_info_box.title: Even if you consume enough calories, too little carbohydrates can accelerate the development of REDs.

screen_10.title: Practical tips to take with you in everyday life
screen_10.subtitle: Tap the circle with the role that best describes you to get simple tips on small choices, routines, and thoughts that are important to have with you in daily preventive work against energy deficiency.

screen_11.title: ATHLETE
list_items1: Eat something every 3 - 4 hours - in addition to after training
Hard to eat right after training? Use a smoothie, energy bar, or drinking yogurt
Note bodily patterns: sleep, energy level, mood, training response, etc.
Speak up if something worries you
Be extras conscious of eating more on the days you train hard

bg_info_box.title: Set up your training week:
bg_info_box.description: Mark which days are hard and which are easy. On hard days you need extra refueling - plan extra meals in advance.

screen_12.title: COACH
screen_12.subtitle: You are a key person for prevention - not just in the training plan, but in the environment you build around the athletes
list_items1: Establish a firm practice for refueling right after training (e.g., encourage bringing a banana, smoothie, bread, etc.)
Encourage athletes to register how their body feels - e.g., through a diary: sleep, food, mood, and performance
Focus on questions that are not only related to performance but also energy levels and response to training
If concerned: collaborate with parents early

bg_info_box.title: Go through the training plan with the athlete:
bg_info_box.description: Which days are heaviest? Where is it extra important to get that extra refueling? Help them connect load with the need for food and rest.

screen_13.title: PARENT
screen_13.subtitle: You are a key person for prevention - you don't need to be an expert, the most important thing is that you pay attention and support
list_items1: Ensure the food amount is adjusted by age, growth, and training volume - many continue with the same lunch box as when they were younger even if the body needs more
Have fixed routines for meals, sleep, and rest
Register changes in mood, sleep patterns, surplus energy, and appetite
Have an open conversation about bodily signals: energy level, sleep, menstrual cycle, morning erections - explain why this says something about health
React early if something feels off - it is easier to adjust than to repair

bg_info_box.title: Sit down and look at the training week together:
bg_info_box.description: When do you train the most? What types of snack foods is it wise to have available on these days?

screen_14.title: Quiz
screen_14.question: What does the body need more of on days with high training load?

screen_15.title: Quiz
screen_15.question: Why is it important to consider when during the day you eat?
screen_15.options1: The body can handle more food in the evening
screen_15.options2: It’s best to eat the most before going to bed
screen_15.options3: Eating early and regularly provides better energy and performance
screen_15.options4: It doesn’t matter—as long as you get enough overall

screen_16.title: Quiz
screen_16.question: What can be a useful strategy to ensure adequate energy intake in daily life?
screen_16.options1: Plan weekly meals only around dinner
screen_16.options2: Track mood and sleep every day
screen_16.options4: Review your training week and identify days that require extra food

screen_01.title: Learning Objectives
screen_01.subtitle: Goals for the module:
list_items1: Understand how to identify and manage REDs
Learn how athletes, coaches, and parents can collaborate
Become familiar with available resources and treatment options

screen_02.title: What now?
screen_02.subtitle: If you think something might not be right - either with yourself or someone you are supporting - it's good to know what you can do. Select your role:

screen_03.title: ATHLETE
screen_03.subtitle1: Step 1: Listen to your body and take the following signs seriously
list_items1: Unusually tired?
Sleeping poorly?
Absence of or irregular menstruation?
Fewer morning erections, strength and 'drive' gone?
Feeling down/poor mood
list_items2: Talk to someone you feel comfortable with

screen_04.title: COACH
list_items1: Athletes with stagnation? Low energy?
list_items2: Talk about sleep, energy, response, normal period (if female)
list_items3: Inform parents - follow-up happens best together
list_items4: Reduce the training load if you are concerned

bg_info_box.title: Did you know:
bg_info_box.description: Low lactate response - especially during threshold and hard training - can indicate REDs. Low carbohydrate access and low energy availability (LEA) affect the ability to produce lactate normally. It is not a certain sign alone but should draw attention - especially together with other symptoms.

screen_05.title: PARENT
screen_05.description: You are a key person for prevention - you don't need to be an expert, the most important thing is that you pay attention and support
list_items1: Feeling down, tired, poor sleep?
Eating less and training more than before?
list_items2: For girls - Do you have your period? Is it regular?
For boys - Have there been changes in morning erections?
list_items3: Contact a GP or specialist, e.g., a nutritionist
list_items4: Enrich the food: use butter, oil, and sauces in meals. Avoid low-fat products.
Snacks, candy, and baked goods are often necessary for sufficient energy intake.

screen_06.title: REDs exists on a spectrum
screen_06.description: The traffic light for energy balance and health in sports

screen_07.title: REDs exists on a spectrum
screen_07.description: The traffic light for energy balance and health in sports

screen_08.title: How does treatment proceed?
screen_08.subtitle: Treating REDs is about helping the body back into balance. This often happens in collaboration with several professionals:

screen_09.title: GP
list_items1: Assesses medical conditions
Orders blood tests
Can refer to a specialist
Can order a DEXA scan (bone density measurement) if reduced bone health is suspected

screen_10.title: CLINICAL NUTRITIONIST
list_items1: Helps to increase energy intake and adapt meal timing
Provides concrete advice for getting enough food - on both normal and more demanding days
Can initiate the need for further investigation, e.g., DEXA scan

screen_12.title: COACH AND PARENTS
screen_12.subtitle1: The coach's role in the treatment phase:
list_items1: Follow up and keep in touch
Contribute to adjusting training
Participate in conversations with the nutritionist if desired
Create security and predictability - the athlete is still part of the team
screen_12.subtitle2: The parents' role in the treatment phase:

sub_list_items1.text: Can be included in conversations with professionals (doctor, nutritionist), if the athlete wishes
sub_items: Increased insight can provide better support in daily life and contribute to better food and rest routines at home

sub_list_items1.text: Important supporter who follows up on signals and adjustments over time

screen_13.title: Resources you can explore
screen_13.description: Athletes with a license through a sports federation can call the Hotline (Sports Health Center) for fast and correct help:
list_items1: Access to a quality-assured treatment network
Specialized expertise in sports medicine
Provides advice regardless of whether the injury is covered by insurance
list_items2: Many good articles, tips, and webinars for athletes, coaches, and parents
list_items3: Fact sheets and guidance on food and meals for athletes

screen_14.title: Quiz
screen_14.question: An athlete shows these signs: lack of progress, often sick, low energy level, stress fracture in the foot, and poor concentration. What is the correct action?
screen_14.options1: Wait and see - it will probably pass
screen_14.options2: Give advice about more sleep and less training for a few weeks
screen_14.options3: Seek out a GP and/or clinical nutritionist
screen_14.options4: Ask the athlete to train lighter and log their own progress

screen_15.title: Quiz
screen_15.question: What should an athlete do if REDs is suspected?
screen_15.options1: Keep it to themselves and see how it goes
screen_15.options2: Talk to an adult and seek professional help
screen_15.options3: Train a bit easier, but do nothing else
screen_15.options4: Search online and try to solve it themselves
